candidate_law: Rescue mechanisms are family-sensitive rather than universal

rows.result_title: Adversarial training improves seen family without broad held-out transfer
rows.boundary_note: Intervention gain was real but structurally narrow rather than broad
rows.comments: Supports family-sensitive gain logic, but was not framed as explicit family-specific rescue architecture

rows.result_title: Explicit carry-conditioned representation rescues only the structurally simplest family
rows.boundary_note: Rescue appears family-limited rather than broad
rows.comments: Strong support: one family rescued, others still fail

rows.result_title: Local context expansion does not rescue other failing families
rows.boundary_note: Simple richer context does not universalize rescue
rows.comments: Supports the idea that rescue is structurally constrained

rows.result_title: Trigger intervention rescues one family but not another
rows.boundary_note: The same local trigger fix is sufficient for one family and insufficient for another
rows.comments: One of the strongest direct supports for Law 3

rows.result_title: Interface and controller rescue different family-level failure modes
rows.boundary_note: No single rescue mechanism handles all tested family types by itself

rows.result_title: Integrated architecture combines family-sensitive rescue mechanisms successfully
rows.boundary_note: Different sub-mechanisms appear necessary for different families

rows.result_title: Cross-family rescue can be harmful while same-family rescue can be safe
rows.boundary_note: Rescue quality depends on family alignment

rows.result_title: Adaptive family-aware rescue outperforms naive cross-family rescue
rows.boundary_note: Adaptive rescue improves because it respects family structure
rows.comments: One of the strongest supports for Law 3

notes: This is the first structured evidence-matrix test in Project 10.
It is meant to replace weak summary-language matching with explicit role-based evidence classification.
